subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: This is a hybrid unit. That means you, the grown-up, lead the learning. The children are 5 to 6 years old. Most of them cannot read many words yet, so you read aloud, you show, and you play together. The child watches, touches, points, sings, and talks. Warmth matters more than long words. Smiles matter more than getting every answer right.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 1 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name the parts of the body: head, arms, legs, hands, feet, and the face parts (eyes, ears, nose, mouth).

Explain simply: every part of the body has a job. Our legs carry us. Our hands hold and make things. Our head keeps our brain safe, and the brain helps us think. Point out that a camel has legs to walk, a bird has wings to fly, and a child has arms and legs to play and work. All bodies are made to move and to do good things.

Explain that our senses are like little helpers that keep us safe. Our eyes see a car coming. Our ears hear someone call our name. Our nose smells smoke. Our skin feels that something is too hot to touch. Allah gave us these senses to help us and protect us. We say Alhamdulillah — thank you Allah — for each one.

concepts.title: Parts of My Body
concepts.explanation: Start with the child's own body. This is the easiest and happiest way to begin, because the science is right there — the child IS the lesson. Stand up together in front of a mirror if you have one.
concepts.example: Touch the top of your head and say the word slowly. Then help the child touch their head.

concepts.title: My Face
concepts.explanation: Now help the child see a big, kind idea: all people have the same body parts, and yet every person looks a little different. Both things are true, and both are wonderful. Look at family photos, or look at two or three people in the home.
concepts.example: Point out what is the same: everyone has a head, two eyes, a nose, two hands, and two feet.

concepts.title: My Five Senses
concepts.explanation: The senses are the ways our body learns about the world. We have five senses. Teach one at a time. Let the child DO each one, not just hear about it. Before you begin, explain the big idea in a simple way. Say: "Our body has special helpers called senses. They tell our brain about the world. We have five of them. Today we will meet each one and try it out." Then spend one short session on each sense, so the child is never rushed. The best way to teach a sense is to USE it — so gather your simple items and let the…
concepts.example: Our eyes let us see colours, shapes, people, and the bright sun. Ask the child to look around and name things they can see: a cup, the door, a green plant. Then ask them to gently close their eyes and try to point to…

concepts.title: Same and Different
concepts.explanation: Now teach the child how to take care of this wonderful body. In Islam, being clean is loved and important. The Prophet (peace be upon him) taught us that cleanliness is a beautiful part of our faith. So washing and keeping clean is both healthy AND a good deed. Go to the sink or a low bowl with a little water and soap.
concepts.example: Wet the hands. Rub in the soap. Make bubbles between the fingers.

concepts.title: Keeping Clean and Healthy
concepts.explanation: Learn about keeping clean and healthy by looking, listening and talking together.

concepts.title: Staying Safe
concepts.explanation: Learn about staying safe by looking, listening and talking together.

activities.title: Taste Test: Sweet or Sour
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of same and different around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Which Nose Knows? Smell Test
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of keeping clean and healthy around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Draw Myself and Label
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of staying safe around your home or outside.
Change one thing, keep everything else the same, and record what happens.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: that at this age, children learn best through their whole body. They learn by touching, moving, singing, and doing — not by sitting still and listening for a long time. So keep every lesson short and full of action. Five to ten minutes of happy play teaches more than half an hour of quiet talking. Stop before the child gets tired, and always end on a smile so they look forward to the next time.

terms: Head
The top part of your body, above your neck.
Arms
The two long parts joined to your shoulders.
Legs
The two long parts you stand and walk on.
Hands
The parts at the end of your arms, used to hold things.
Feet
The parts at the end of your legs that you stand on.
Eyes
The body part we use to see.
Ears
The body part we use to hear.
Nose
The body part we use to smell.
Mouth
The body part we use to taste and to speak.
Senses
The five ways we learn about the world: see, hear, smell, taste and touch.

vocabulary.meaning: The top part of your body, above your neck.

selfAssessment: I can name the parts of the body: head, arms, legs, hands, feet, and the face parts (eyes, ears, nose, mouth).

I can explain simply: every part of the body has a job. Our legs carry us. Our hands hold and make things. Our head keeps our brain safe, and the brain helps us think. Point out that a camel has legs to walk, a bird has wings to fly, and a child has arms and legs to play and work. All bodies are made to move and to do good things.

I can explain that our senses are like little helpers that keep us safe. Our eyes see a car coming. Our ears hear someone call our name. Our nose smells smoke. Our skin feels that something is too hot to touch. Allah gave us these senses to help us and protect us. We say Alhamdulillah — thank you Allah — for each one.
